findings.description: yaml.load(f) is called without SafeLoader, so a crafted config file can execute arbitrary Python (!!python/object/apply) — a textbook RCE in something literally named a config loader.
Precedence is inverted versus the docstring: env is applied then file via dict.update, so the file overrides env (last-write-wins), making the promised 'env > file > defaults' contract a lie.
feature_enabled assumes feature_flags is a dict; if the file or an APP_FEATURE_FLAGS env var sets it to a scalar, flags.get raises AttributeError instead of returning False.
require() cannot distinguish an absent key from a key whose legitimate value is None — both raise KeyError — so a real None-valued config entry is unrepresentable.
Every get()/require()/feature_enabled() call invokes load(), which re-reads the file from disk, re-walks os.environ, and re-logs — no caching, so N accesses mean N disk reads.
Env overlay flat-lowercases keys and coerces scalars, so APP_FEATURE_FLAGS silently replaces the nested dict default with a string, corrupting the shape feature_enabled depends on.
open() on config_path follows symlinks and reads any path the process can reach with no path/permission validation, enabling info disclosure if config_path is attacker-influenced.
Logging 'config loaded' at INFO on every load() (compounded by the no-cache reload bug) produces high-volume log spam under repeated get() calls.
